text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY SURVEILLANCE VISIT
Head and Neck Oncology Program

PATIENT: Margaret Chen
AGE: 54 years
DIAGNOSIS: HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma, right base of tongue, Stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th ed.)
TREATMENT SUMMARY: Definitive concurrent chemoradiation — IMRT 70 Gy in 35 fractions (SIB technique) + cisplatin 100 mg/m2 x2 cycles. Treatment completed approximately 24 months ago.

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Routine 24-month post-treatment surveillance visit. Patient presents for scheduled follow-up. No acute complaints.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen is a 54-year-old Chinese-American never-smoking female with a history of p16-positive, HPV-16-associated squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue (T2N1M0) who completed definitive concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiation approximately 24 months ago. She achieved a complete metabolic response on post-treatment PET/CT at 12 weeks, and has remained without radiographic or clinical evidence of disease on all subsequent surveillance imaging.

Today she presents for her 2-year surveillance landmark appointment. She denies any new symptoms concerning for disease recurrence. Specifically, she reports no new or enlarging neck masses, no worsening odynophagia or dysphagia, no hemoptysis, no unexplained weight loss, no otalgia, and no trismus. She has not noticed any new lumps or bumps in the neck or jaw region. She denies fevers, night sweats, or significant fatigue.

Her late treatment effects continue to be manageable and are largely unchanged since her last visit six months ago. She continues to experience grade 1 xerostomia, for which she uses saliva substitutes (Biotene spray) and sips water frequently throughout the day. She notes this remains a nuisance but is not debilitating. She is tolerating a regular diet with only minor modifications — she tends to avoid very dry foods such as crackers or crusty bread without liquid accompaniment, and occasionally has minor difficulty with very tough or fibrous meat. Oral intake is otherwise unrestricted. She has no resting dysphagia and manages most food textures without difficulty.

Her PEG tube was removed approximately 19 months ago without complication, and she has fully resumed oral nutrition. Her weight has been stable near her pre-treatment baseline.

She reports that she has returned to full-time work as a high school music teacher and is engaged professionally and socially. She describes her quality of life as good. She does note mild hoarseness that she attributes partly to her xerostomia and partly to the demands of her work (extensive voice use), but she has not sought formal evaluation for this and denies progressive worsening.

Hypothyroidism: Her levothyroxine dose was increased approximately 18 months ago after TSH was found to be mildly elevated, consistent with radiation-induced hypothyroidism. She is currently on an adjusted dose and TSH has been normal on repeat checks per her primary care physician.

Hearing: She was counseled at prior visits regarding mild cisplatin-related high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (4–8 kHz bilaterally). She states this has not progressed perceptibly and is not affecting her professional functioning; she has not required hearing aids. She continues with annual audiologic monitoring.

Dental: She is compliant with daily fluoride tray use and sees her dentist every 6 months. No issues with dental decay or jaw pain reported; she has had no complications related to prior radiation.

She has no complaints referable to the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. No new skin lesions noted by patient.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: Mild xerostomia (chronic, stable, grade 1). Mild high-frequency hearing change (bilateral, stable). Occasional minor difficulty with very dry food textures.
Negative: No new neck masses. No throat pain or significant odynophagia. No hemoptysis or blood-tinged saliva. No unexplained weight change. No trismus or new jaw stiffness. No otalgia. No voice change beyond mild hoarseness from baseline. No shortness of breath. No chest pain. No abdominal pain. No neurological symptoms. No fevers, chills, or night sweats. No bone pain.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Healthy-appearing for stated age. Engaged and interactive.
Vital Signs: Weight 61 kg (within 1–2 lbs of pre-treatment baseline). BP 118/74 mmHg. HR 72 bpm, regular. Temperature 37.0°C. SpO2 99% on room air.
Performance Status: ECOG 0.

Head and Neck:
- Face and scalp: No skin lesions or masses. No parotid fullness.
- Oral cavity: Mucosa moist with slightly reduced salivary pooling consistent with residual radiation-induced xerostomia. No mucosal lesions, ulcers, or masses. No oral thrush. Dentition intact and well-maintained. No frank trismus — mouth opening adequate (approximately 4 cm interincisal distance).
- Oropharynx (flexible nasopharyngoscopy performed in conjunction with ENT at this visit): Right base of tongue demonstrates well-healed, smooth mucosa without visible mass, ulceration, or concerning mucosal irregularity. Left base of tongue normal. Tonsils absent (note: tonsils not surgically removed — prior procedure was dental extraction only; residual tonsillar tissue appears atrophic, consistent with radiation effect). Soft palate mobile and symmetric. Posterior pharyngeal wall without lesions. Larynx: vocal cords mobile bilaterally, mild posterior glottic erythema, no masses. Subglottis and supraglottis unremarkable.
- Neck: No palpable cervical, submandibular, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy bilaterally. Mild fibrotic induration in the right level II region consistent with post-treatment changes, unchanged from prior examinations. Skin of bilateral anterior and posterior neck shows residual post-radiation hyperpigmentation without telangiectasias, ulceration, or other concerning skin changes.
- Thyroid: Not palpably enlarged. No nodules appreciated.

Chest: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. Gastrostomy site well-healed, no erythema or granuloma.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No clubbing.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. No focal deficits.

---

LABORATORY / DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS:
CT Neck with Contrast (obtained prior to today's visit, reviewed today):
Post-radiation changes in the right base of tongue and oropharynx stable and unchanged. Soft tissue stranding and mucosal thickening consistent with expected post-treatment sequelae. No discrete mass, abnormal nodular enhancement, or soft tissue fullness to suggest recurrence. Right level IIA lymph node now 0.5–0.6 cm with homogeneous enhancement, stable to slightly decreased compared to 18-month imaging. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in any cervical station bilaterally. No new lesions in the included chest and upper abdominal cuts. IMPRESSION: No evidence of locoregional or distant disease recurrence. Stable post-radiation changes.

Thyroid function (per PCP, obtained 6 weeks ago): TSH 2.1 mIU/L — within normal limits on current levothyroxine dose.

Audiogram (per audiology, 3 months ago): Mild bilateral high-frequency SNHL (4–8 kHz), stable compared to prior testing. No low-frequency progression. No intervention required at this time.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HPV-POSITIVE OROPHARYNGEAL SCC, RIGHT BASE OF TONGUE — STAGE III (T2N1M0), IN COMPLETE REMISSION:
Ms. Chen has now reached the 24-month disease-free surveillance milestone following definitive concurrent chemoradiation. This is a highly favorable landmark for this disease. She has no clinical, endoscopic, or radiographic evidence of locoregional or distant disease recurrence. She has achieved and maintained a sustained complete response. I had an extended discussion with her today about the significance of this milestone: for patients with HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC who achieve a complete response and remain disease-free at 2 years, the risk of late relapse continues to decline substantially, and long-term (5-year) overall survival rates in her subgroup exceed 85% in contemporary series. She was visibly reassured by this information.

No further treatment is indicated. She will continue NCCN-concordant surveillance per the HPV-positive OPSCC protocol. Per NCCN guidelines for years 3–5 post-treatment, surveillance visits will now be every 2–6 months. I will see her in approximately 4 months; she should also continue follow-up with Dr. Park (radiation oncology) per that team's schedule.

I have counseled her to present promptly — without waiting for her next scheduled visit — if she develops any of the following: a new or enlarging neck mass, unexplained persistent throat pain or odynophagia, hemoptysis, frank voice change, new otalgia, or unexplained weight loss exceeding 5 lbs over a short period. She verbalized understanding.

Imaging plan: Routine annual CT neck with contrast per NCCN guidelines. No PET/CT routinely indicated at this interval in the absence of clinical concern; will obtain if clinical examination or CT raises concern.

2. RADIATION-INDUCED XEROSTOMIA (GRADE 1, CHRONIC):
Stable. Continues Biotene spray and adequate hydration. Discussed potential for some further gradual recovery of salivary function over time, though complete resolution unlikely. Will continue amifostine was not used during her treatment; pilocarpine 5 mg TID is currently listed as a medication — confirm she is still taking and assess benefit at next visit. Continued daily fluoride tray use reinforced.

3. RADIATION-INDUCED HYPOTHYROIDISM:
TSH normalized on adjusted levothyroxine dose, managed by PCP. No changes required from oncology perspective. Will continue annual TSH monitoring.

4. CISPLATIN-RELATED SENSORINEURAL HEARING LOSS (BILATERAL, HIGH-FREQUENCY, MILD):
Stable on most recent audiogram. Not functionally limiting at this time. She is a music teacher and is understandably attentive to any progression; she has not noticed subjective worsening. Annual audiology follow-up ongoing; she should continue to report any new subjective changes between scheduled assessments.

5. LATE RADIATION EFFECTS — CAROTID SURVEILLANCE:
In light of bilateral neck irradiation, patient was counseled at prior visits regarding the long-term risk of radiation-induced carotid artery disease. She is a never-smoker with no other significant cardiovascular risk factors. PCP is aware. Recommend that primary care physician assess carotid health as part of ongoing cardiovascular monitoring; carotid duplex imaging may be considered per vascular surgery or PCP guidance in the coming years.

6. DENTAL HEALTH:
Patient is compliant with twice-yearly dental evaluations and daily fluoride tray use — excellent adherence. Reinforced to notify the dental team of any jaw pain, tooth pain, or non-healing oral lesion and to ensure any future dental procedures are communicated to the treating oncology team given prior mandibular radiation field.

7. RETURN TO FUNCTIONAL BASELINE:
Patient has returned to full-time professional activities as a music teacher and reports excellent quality of life. Continued supportive care and monitoring as outlined above. Nutritional status excellent; weight at pre-treatment baseline.

Return to clinic in approximately 4 months for next surveillance visit.

Total visit time: 40 minutes, more than 50% spent in counseling and care coordination.

Attending Physician: [Medical Oncology, Head and Neck Program]
